user_prompt: We will work on `lib\FlyGuiViews\RecordingView`, I've created some files inside with names that indicate their purpose
`RecordingView.cpp` itself will be used in two ways, one is for a call/meeting recording over bluetooth, and the other is a local memo recording
For a call/meeting, the speaker button is available, and there is a "answer call" button available.

For a local memo recording, there is no speaker button, and also no "answer call", but there is a button that can change the memo type (for things like: todo, journal, idea, etc). Cycle through the `sprit_memotype_` sprites
Both of these modes use the same layout.

There is always a red border, use four filled rectangles for this, the border is 10 pixels thick, drawing a box starting from x=0 y=10 (because we still want to see the top bar, do not move the top bar) down to the bottom right corner

Along the bottom of the screen, we now have 300 pixel wide area to work with because of two 10px thick borders, this is divided into three 100x100 pixel buttons. These buttons are also linked to the dedicated buttons `BtnA` `BtnB` and `BtnC`.

`AudioDeviceButton` can either be for a mic or a speaker. For a mic button, touching it will activate the mic for push-to-talk, touching it again will cause it to go back to speaker mode. The mic icon will be fully bright when in mic mode, and faded, with a red X draw over it when not in mic mode. For a speaker button, it is faded when in mic mode, and not faded when in speaker mode. There is also a speaker muted mode that is the not-faded button but with a red X drawn over it. When in speaker mode, touching the speaker button toggles speaker mute.
The drawing of the red X is a shared feature, draw the `sprit_muted_x` over the center of the button, it's a square so don't need to care about opacity.

Another shared feature: The top row of pixels is a audio level meter reflecting the peak levels. The line grows from the center outwards. When the peak level is at saturation, the entire row of pixels is fully red. When the level is 0, no pixels show. A low level is a short line in the center that is white. The colour reflects the level, Red is fully saturated, white is low or 0, yellow is in the middle.

The first of the three buttons is the mic button `sprit_btn_mic`, then the speaker button `sprit_btn_speaker` (if required), the third one will be a exit button `sprit_btn_end` that ends the recording.
The first coordinate is x=10 y=130, the second is x=110 y=130, the third is x=210 y=130
`RecordingView` is the owner of the code that changes the state of the buttons, not the buttons themselves
For the answer call button or the memo type button, the coordinate is x=260 y=70, size is 50x50, use `sprit_pickup` and `sprit_pickupdown`
If in bluetooth mode, there will be a static non-interactive sprite at x=260 y=20 that is also 50x50, use `sprit_bluetooth_50`
The icon inside "answer call" button changes according to `PhoneUiState CallManager::uiState()`. use `sprit_pickup` and `sprit_pickupdown`

Starting from x=14 y=101, there will be several lines of text. The first line is the recording file name, this is static. The second line should show how long the recording has been going on for, which needs the `lib\FlyGui\FlyGuiText.cpp`, the third line also uses `FlyGuiText` and shows all `getCallerInfoAt` items, one item at a time, cycling to the next one every 3 seconds.
For all the functions that need to control (so I don't mean just using getters) other modules like AudioManager or AudioFileRecorder or BtManager or etc, use `RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp`
This is a long instruction, warn me about mistakes
agent_reply: - Added Bluetooth vs memo modes with shared layout, red border, bottom button mapping, caller-info cycling, elapsed time, filename text, and center-out audio meter.
- Added `RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp` for external control of `AudioManager`, `AudioFileRecorder`, and `BtManager`.
- Replaced the old registered `ActiveRecordingView` with the new `RecordingView` in `src/all_init.cpp`.
- Fixed the view redraw path so clearing the frame forces all child sprites/text back onto the screen.

Warnings I’d flag: the audio meter is drawn at `y=20`, inside the top red border, because `y=10` conflicts with the required always-red border and the visible top bar. Also, changing memo type after recording has started currently changes the icon only; the file/type is chosen when recording begins.
Build passes. `src/version.c` was regenerated by the build.

user_prompt: if `gui->showView(FLYGUI_VIEW_MAIN)` happens after `bt_host_list->loadFromMicroSd()`, why does it seem like it took time for the phone and laptop items to load?
agent_reply: Yes, your instinct is right, but there’s a second thing making it look delayed.

`MainScreenView` is constructed early as a static in [all_init.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:32), before `setup()` creates and loads `bt_host_list`. Its constructor calls `syncBluetoothHostButtonFades()`, and at that point `bt_host_list == nullptr`, so phone/laptop start faded.
But the bigger issue is this: [SplashView.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/SplashView.cpp:31) automatically calls:
after the splash delay. That happens inside `show_splash()`, which currently runs before:
So the main view is actually shown once before the Bluetooth JSON is loaded.

`FlyGui::showView()` sees that main is already the current view and returns immediately without `onLoad()` or forced redraw. Then a later normal poll/redraw finally runs `MainScreenView::redraw()`, calls `syncBluetoothHostButtonFades()`, and the icons catch up.

So the real cause is not just early construction. It’s that `SplashView` hands off to main too early, and the later explicit `showView(MAIN)` becomes a no-op. Best fix later: remove the splash view’s automatic transition and let `setup()` be the only place that transitions to main after all boot data is loaded.
